reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。
request_prompt.user:
/no_think
下面是一个 FNM unresolved cluster。请只返回 JSON 数组，不要解释。
本次只允许动作：match、ignore_ref、synthesize_note_item、needs_review。
若截图已经能明确判断坏掉的数字或同页注释，请直接输出可自动落地的动作，不要退回 needs_review。
若截图里清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化数据里没有 note item，优先用 synthesize_note_item；marker 必须是截图上可见的数字，note_text 只抄注释正文，不要自编，不要补全看不清的部分。

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In addition to painting a more complete image of medical views of madness, this broad

Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子).

I start by examining the prototypical symptoms of kuang as defined in the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (first century BCE), a pivotal corpus from which medical writers drew their descriptions of madness for most of imperial history. I then criticize the way modern scholars have used "mental illness" as a foil against which to define Chinese approaches, or even as a reason to refuse comparisons between psychiatry and Chinese medicine. As I shall argue, Chinese medicine and modern psychiatry are comparable because both purport to explain and treat psycho-behavioral symptoms. As I explain next, Chinese doctors assumed that mental activities "supervened" upon organic processes. $ ^{1} $ This is the most critical feature of Chinese medical approaches to insanity. I then map the relations between kuang and proximate illnesses in a seventh-century treatise that attributed most psycho-behavioral pathologies to harmful Winds. The fourth section explains how dian (瑱, 顛, or 癲)—"seizure sickness"—came to refer to insanity by borrowing from non-medical usage.

Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen in time" (Lo 2002: xxix).

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity.

rebind_candidates.note_excerpt: to 6. $ ^{3} $ I adapt this turn of phrase from Colin Jones 1990: 379.
rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Throughout, I use “illness” as a translation of the term bing 病 and as a label for the kinds of disorders that Chinese medicine discussed: clusters of clinically relevant symptoms that were grouped under one particular name. The problem in translating bing is not to determine whether that term really meant “illness,” “disease,” or “disorder,” but to find a rendering that neither clashes with the meaning of bing nor brings undue connotations into our analysis. To some extent, a translation is good because of what it avoids. Translating bing as “illness” will not presuppose that all bing defined in Chinese medical texts were “diseases,” a term that now seems to imply rootedness in biological processes. I also avoid “disorder” because it would make Chinese concepts of madness sound too close to those of the DSM (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders), the influential manual that calls most psychiatric derangements “disorders” (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder and the like). $ ^{4} $

I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen ...

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity. One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵ When this [vessel] is moved, one ails from: shivers and ailing from coldness; fondness for stretching; continual yawning, blackness in the center of the forehead; ailing from swelling; when severe, detestation for people and fire; when hearing the sound of wood, panicked alarm; panicky heart; the desire to close doors and windows and dwell in isolation; when the ailment is severe, the desire to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run. These constitute shin reversal. For these [ailments], the Yang-Brilliance vessel controls the treatment.

是勤〔動〕則病 洒洒 病塞＜寒＞ 喜信〔伸〕 數吹〔欠〕 嬿〔顏〕 墨 病種〔腫〕 至則惡人與火 聞木音則狄〔惕〕 然驚 心惕然欲獨必戶牖而處 病甚則欲乘高而歌 棄衣而走 此為骭蹶〔厥〕 是陽明脈主治 # Note the seamless transition between what we could call physical and behavioral symptoms. $ ^{6} $ # Two passages from the Basic Questions appear to have been comments on the above. Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $ “When the illness is serious, [one] discards clothes and runs about, mounts heights and sings. One may even not eat for several days. One will leap walls and ascend rooftops. One would not be able to climb [in such a way] ordinarily, yet when one is ill one unexpectedly can. Why is this?” Qibo said: “The four limbs are the root of all Yang [vessels]. When Yang is flourishing, the four limbs are replete, which makes one able to climb high.” The Yellow Emperor said: “Why does one shed clothes and run?” Qibo said: “When Heat is flourishing in the body, one sheds clothes and wants to run.” The Yellow Emperor: “Why d ...
